planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter, spring, or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Chinese pistache tolerates drought, alkaline soil, clay, and reflected heat — making it one of the most adaptable shade trees for Gulf Coast urban conditions.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Chinese pistache is widely regarded as the single best fall-color deciduous shade tree for the Gulf South — one of the very few trees that reliably produces brilliant orange, scarlet, and red fall color in zone 9, where autumn temperatures are too mild for most fall-color trees to perform. While maples, oaks, and sweetgums produce muted or inconsistent fall color in zone 9, Chinese pistache reliably transitions to vivid orange-red in late October through November even with the mild, late arrival of cool nights in the Gulf Coast region. The tree grows 30–40 feet tall with a broad, rounded canopy that provides excellent shade, and it is well-suited to the challenging conditions common in Gulf Coast urban landscapes — clay soils, alkaline soil pH, extended summer drought, reflected heat from pavement, and periodic wet periods all fail to deter established Chinese pistache. Female trees produce ornamental clusters of small red berries that transition to blue-purple in winter and are attractive to birds. Trees are dioecious (separate male and female trees); most nursery trees are grafted males selected for form, or named females. Exceptionally low-maintenance once established — no serious pest or disease problems affect Chinese pistache in the Gulf South.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter, spring, or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Forest Pansy does not transplant well when large — purchase and plant small container trees and disturb the root system as little as possible.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Forest Pansy is the most widely grown and recognized purple-leafed redbud cultivar — selected for its spectacular, rich purple to wine-red heart-shaped leaves that emerge in spring immediately after the rosy-pink flower display and persist with deep purple coloration through summer, transitioning to orange-red in fall. The stunning spring sequence begins with the bare branches covered in rosy-pink flowers in February–March in zone 9 (before the leaves emerge), followed immediately by the emergence of the deep wine-red heart-shaped leaves — a foliage display that maintains dramatic interest throughout the growing season. The purple leaf color is most intense in spring and early summer, gradually fading to greenish-purple through the hottest summer months, then reviving to orange-red in fall. Forest Pansy grows as a small multi-trunked or single-stemmed tree to 15–25 feet tall and wide, with an elegant horizontal branching pattern. It is more shade-tolerant than many ornamental trees, performing well with 4–6 hours of sun in the understory of larger trees, though leaf color is most vibrant in full sun. In zone 9, afternoon shade helps prevent summer leaf scorch. Plant in well-drained soil — redbud roots are highly susceptible to root rot in poorly drained, wet sites.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter, spring, or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Oklahoma redbud is particularly well-adapted to alkaline limestone soils of the Edwards Plateau and to the heavy clay soils of the Gulf Coastal Plain.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Oklahoma redbud is a Texas-native botanical variety of eastern redbud that is substantially more heat- and drought-tolerant than the standard eastern redbud (Cercis canadensis), making it the superior choice for zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens where summer heat and drought stress often cause the standard species to decline. The cultivar 'Oklahoma' was selected for exceptional dark rose-magenta to rosy-purple flower color — deeper and more saturated than the typical light pink-lavender of species redbud — and for glossy, leathery, rich dark-green leaves that are waxy-looking and more heat-resistant than the soft, matte leaves of the standard species. The flower display appears on bare branches in February–March in zone 9 and is spectacular — the vivid magenta-rose flowers cover the branches in dense clusters before the leaves emerge. The glossy-leaved foliage provides excellent summer interest and greater resistance to summer leaf drop than standard eastern redbud. Oklahoma redbud is smaller and more compact than the eastern species, reaching 12–20 feet tall and wide, making it appropriate for smaller residential landscapes. Superior drought tolerance, heat resistance, and adaptability to alkaline and clay soils make Oklahoma redbud one of the finest ornamental trees for zone 9.

common_name: Bloodgood Japanese Maple (Red Upright Japanese Maple)

planting.note: Plant container trees in late winter or early spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile, humus-rich soil in dappled shade to partial shade; in zone 9, afternoon shade is essential to prevent leaf scorch — morning sun with afternoon shade is ideal for Bloodgood in hot-summer climates.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile, humus-rich soil in dappled to partial shade; Bloodgood is the single most widely planted and most reliable upright red-leafed Japanese maple cultivar in American horticulture — selected for excellent dark reddish-purple leaf retention through summer (resisting the tendency of many red-leaf maples to fade to greenish-bronze in summer heat), attractive upright-spreading form reaching 12–18 feet, and reliable deep scarlet-red fall color. The five-lobed, deeply cut leaves emerge rich reddish-purple in spring and hold good color through the growing season with adequate moisture and afternoon shade. Bloodgood's vertical-upright branching pattern creates an elegant, architecturally refined form that provides seasonal interest year-round — spring leaf emergence, summer purple foliage, scarlet fall color, and attractive branching structure in winter. In zone 9, Bloodgood requires careful siting with afternoon shade, moist but well-drained soil, and protection from hot dry winds to prevent summer leaf scorch. It performs reliably as a landscape tree in zone 9 with correct placement — east-facing exposures or locations under tall trees providing dappled afternoon shade are ideal. Consistent moisture during summer heat stress periods is the key to success in zone 9. The reddish-purple bark on young stems adds winter interest.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Debutante is best planted in fall in zone 9 so the roots establish during the cool season before facing summer heat — avoid planting in summer.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Debutante is one of the most beloved and widely grown Camellia japonica cultivars — a long-cultivated Southern garden classic producing early to mid-season (December through February in zone 9) flowers in a gorgeous soft light-pink peony form with rows of neatly layered petals that create a formal, rose-like, fully double flower of exceptional elegance. The soft light-pink color — a warm blush to light rose — makes Debutante one of the most feminine and refined camellias for traditional Southern landscape and cut-flower use. The upright, vigorous growth to 6–12 feet tall with dense, glossy dark-green evergreen foliage makes it an outstanding foundation shrub and informal hedge plant for shaded or partially shaded locations. Debutante is known for reliable, abundant bloom production even in zone 9's mild winters — bloom quality does not depend on cold temperatures to trigger flower set. In zone 9, Debutante typically begins blooming in late November and continues through February, often with peak bloom in January. Plant in partial shade — morning sun is acceptable but afternoon shade is essential in zone 9 to prevent sun scorch of the glossy leaves. Mulch heavily with pine bark or pine straw to protect the shallow, fibrous root system and maintain the acidic soil conditions camellias require.
